narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 47% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, the model zeroed in on a few key elements from your profile: your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which sits just below the 3.24 average for those who passed; your LSAT score of 24, notably lower than the 37.27 average among successful bar takers; your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles both at 7, which are solid but higher than the 6.04 and 5.91 averages for passers; and your middle family income bracket, which aligns with about 35% of those who succeeded but less common than upper-middle incomes. The single biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your LSAT score, as it stood out as a strong signal of potential challenges on the bar compared to typical admitted students. On a more positive note, your Year 3 grade decile had the second-strongest influence, pulling the prediction toward success because it showed improvement and ranked well against passers. Your Year 1 decile also helped by nudging things in a favorable direction, though with less impact. Overall, these stacked up such that your profile resembled more students who struggled on the bar than those who passed and got admitted—especially with full-time status being common among successes, which fits you but couldn't outweigh the rest. It's tough to hear, especially with your consistent performance in later years and background that mirrors many who make it through. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the typical 37 range, as it carries the most weight; strengthening your academic deciles further through additional coursework could help too. You might also explore programs with bar passage support or part-time options if that aligns better. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting these areas—keep pushing, as your upward trajectory in grades suggests real potential.
